title: Using Emissions Control (EMCON) Effectively
body:
Emissions Control (EMCON) is one of the most powerful — and most overlooked — tools in CMO. When a unit is set to EMCON, it stops broadcasting radar and radio signals, making it invisible to enemy ESM sensors.

Why it matters: Modern warfare is largely about detection. The side that detects first, shoots first. If your ships or aircraft are broadcasting radar, enemy ESM platforms (aircraft, ships, satellites) will detect them at ranges far beyond your own radar coverage.

How to use it:
1. Keep high-value units (carriers, command ships, SSBNs) on EMCON at all times unless actively needed.
2. Use passive sensors (ESM, sonar) to build your picture without emitting.
3. Only go active (radar on) when you need to prosecute a contact — and accept that you will now be detected.
4. Aircraft performing CAP can stay on EMCON until they get a datalink track from an AWACS.

Common mistake: New players leave radar on everything at all times. This telegraphs your entire order of battle to the enemy within minutes of scenario start.

title: How to Set Up a CAP Station
body:
A Combat Air Patrol (CAP) station is a defined patrol area where fighters orbit waiting to intercept incoming threats. Setting one up correctly in CMO makes a huge difference in how quickly your fighters respond.

Steps:
1. Select your fighter and assign a Patrol mission (Mission Editor > Add Mission > Patrol/CAP).
2. Draw the patrol zone well forward of your assets — you want to intercept threats before they reach weapons release range.
3. Set the patrol altitude high (25,000–40,000 ft) for maximum radar coverage and fuel efficiency.
4. Assign at least 2 aircraft per station so one is always on station while the other refuels.
5. Enable 'Maintain CAP' so the game automatically cycles replacements.

Tip: Position your CAP station so the fighters have a tail-wind when intercepting — this extends their effective range and shortens intercept time.

title: Why Submarines Win: Staying Undetected
body:
Submarines are the most powerful platforms in CMO when used correctly — and completely useless when used wrong. The single most important rule: never go fast.

Speed and noise: A submarine moving above 5–7 knots generates enormous cavitation noise, detectable by enemy sonar at long range. Stay below 5 knots when in contested waters.

Depth: Use layer depth to your advantage. The thermocline (a temperature boundary layer in the ocean) refracts sound and creates a 'shadow zone' that hides you from surface sonars. Dive below the layer when evading, come above it when hunting surface ships.

Attack procedure:
1. Detect a surface contact passively (towed array or hull sonar).
2. Slowly maneuver to a firing position — ideally within 10–15 nm.
3. Fire torpedoes or anti-ship missiles and immediately go deep and change course.
4. Sprint away at full speed after firing — you have ~2 minutes before the enemy locates you.

Common mistake: Using active sonar to find targets. Active sonar tells every ship and aircraft in 50nm exactly where you are.
